ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock IV
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all possible buy/sell sequences with at most k transactions.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force recursive solution to the problem. The intuition is to explore every possible sequence of buy and sell actions on the given price array, while respecting the constraint of at most k transactions. We use recursion to simulate two states at each day: either we're holding a stock (bought previously) or we're not holding any stock. When not holding, we can either buy (starting a new transaction) or skip. When holding, we can either sell (completing a transaction) or skip. Each transaction consists of a buy followed by a sell. We track the number of transactions completed so far and ensure it doesn't exceed k. The base cases are when we reach the end of the price array or when we've used all k transactions. The algorithm explores all 2^n possibilities in the worst case, making it exponential. The space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack depth. This approach connects to L1 by using the recursive helper function as its core component. Common mistakes include forgetting to track the holding state, incorrectly counting transactions, or not handling base cases properly. Real code pattern: def maxProfit(prices, k, day=0, holding=False, transactions=0): if day == len(prices) or transactions == k: return 0; if not holding: buy = -prices[day] + maxProfit(prices, k, day+1, True, transactions); skip = maxProfit(prices, k, day+1, False, transactions); return max(buy, skip); else: sell = prices[day] + maxProfit(prices, k, day+1, False, transactions+1); skip = maxProfit(prices, k, day+1, True, transactions); return max(sell, skip).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Helper Function for Stock Transactions
ladders.desc: Recursive function that explores buy/sell decisions at each day with state tracking.
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive helper function used in the full solution. The intuition is to model the problem as a state machine where at each day, we're either holding a stock or not, and we have a count of completed transactions. The function recursively explores two choices at each state: if not holding, we can buy (transition to holding state) or skip (stay not holding); if holding, we can sell (transition to not holding and increment transaction count) or skip (stay holding). The base cases are when we reach beyond the last day or when the transaction count reaches k, returning 0 profit. The algorithm uses depth-first search to explore all possible sequences. Time complexity is O(2^n) in the worst case as it explores all combinations. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. This connects to L0 by being the main recursive engine, and to L2 as it implements the core logic of state transitions. Common mistakes include incorrect base case conditions, not tracking the holding state properly, or off-by-one errors in transaction counting. Real code pattern: def helper(day, holding, transactions): if day == n or transactions == k: return 0; if not holding: buy = -prices[day] + helper(day+1, True, transactions); skip = helper(day+1, False, transactions); return max(buy, skip); else: sell = prices[day] + helper(day+1, False, transactions+1); skip = helper(day+1, True, transactions); return max(sell, skip).

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Transitions for Buy/Sell Decisions
ladders.desc: Understanding the decision tree and state changes at each day.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core logic of state transitions in the recursive approach. The intuition is that at any given day, your profit-maximizing decision depends on whether you currently hold a stock or not, and how many transactions you've completed. The state is defined by three variables: current day, holding status (boolean), and transaction count. From each state, you have two possible actions: if not holding, you can buy (which costs the current price and changes state to holding) or skip (stay not holding); if holding, you can sell (which gains the current price, changes to not holding, and increments transaction count) or skip (stay holding). This creates a binary decision tree where each path represents a sequence of buy/sell actions. The goal is to find the path with maximum net profit. The algorithm uses recursion to explore this tree, with base cases cutting off exploration when we run out of days or transactions. Time complexity arises from the exponential number of paths. Space is linear due to recursion depth. This connects to L1 as it defines the recursive function's structure, and to L3 which handles the base cases. Common mistakes include confusing when to increment transactions, or not properly modeling the holding state. Real code pattern: At each recursive call, check holding status; if not holding: profit_buy = -price[day] + recurse(day+1, True, trans); profit_skip = recurse(day+1, False, trans); return max(profit_buy, profit_skip). If holding: profit_sell = price[day] + recurse(day+1, False, trans+1); profit_skip = recurse(day+1, True, trans); return max(profit_sell, profit_skip).

ladders.title: Building Block — Base Cases and Termination Conditions
ladders.desc: Handling edge cases when recursion must stop: end of array or transaction limit reached.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the base cases that terminate the recursion. The intuition is that we must stop exploring further when it's impossible to make more profitable moves: either we've reached the end of the price array (no more days to trade), or we've used all allowed transactions (k). In both cases, no further profit can be earned, so we return 0. Specifically, if day == n (where n is the length of prices), we're beyond the last day and cannot perform any actions. If transactions == k, we've completed the maximum number of transactions allowed, so we cannot sell any held stock (since selling would require starting a new transaction) or buy new stock (as buying would eventually require selling, exceeding k). The algorithm checks these conditions at the start of each recursive call. Time complexity is O(1) for this check, but it affects the overall recursion depth. Space is O(1) for the condition check. This connects to L2 as it defines when state transitions stop, and to L4 which handles individual day operations. Common mistakes include forgetting to check both conditions, or incorrectly allowing actions when transactions == k (e.g., selling might still be allowed if it doesn't increase count, but in this model, selling increments count). Real code pattern: if day >= len(prices) or transactions >= k: return 0.

Transactions=2 is equal to k=2, so we've used all allowed transactions. Base case triggers: return 0 profit because no more transactions can be made, even though there are remaining days (day=1 out of 3).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Profit Calculation for a Single Buy/Sell Action
ladders.desc: Computing the profit impact of buying or selling on a specific day.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental operation of calculating profit change from a single buy or sell action. The intuition is simple: buying a stock reduces your profit by the purchase price (since you pay money), and selling a stock increases your profit by the sale price (since you receive money). In the context of the recursive algorithm, when we decide to buy at a given day, we subtract the price at that day from the current profit tally. When we decide to sell, we add the price at that day. These operations are performed at each recursive step to accumulate total profit along a path. The algorithm uses these calculations in the recursive calls: for a buy action, profit = -prices[day] + recursive_call(...); for a sell action, profit = prices[day] + recursive_call(...). Time complexity is O(1) per operation. Space is O(1). This connects to L3 as it's used within the recursive function before checking base cases, and to L5 which involves basic arithmetic. Common mistakes include incorrect signs (e.g., adding for buy or subtracting for sell), or using the wrong day index. Real code pattern: buy_profit = -prices[current_day]; sell_profit = +prices[current_day].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Profit as Sum of Differences
ladders.desc: Basic idea that total profit is the sum of individual buy/sell gains.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept: in stock trading, your total profit is the sum of profits from each completed transaction, where each transaction consists of buying at a lower price and selling at a higher price. For a single transaction, profit = sell_price - buy_price. If you make multiple transactions, total profit = sum of (sell_price_i - buy_price_i) for all i. This is intuitive because each time you buy and sell, you gain the difference. In the recursive approach, we accumulate these differences along the path of decisions. The algorithm doesn't explicitly pair buys and sells; instead, it tracks net profit by adding sell prices and subtracting buy prices as decisions are made. This concept is prerequisite to understanding how the recursive function builds up profit. Time and space are not applicable here as it's a conceptual understanding. This connects to L4 as it explains why we add for sells and subtract for buys. Common mistakes include thinking of profit as a ratio or percentage, or not realizing that profit can be negative if you sell at a lower price than you bought. Real code pattern: Not code, but mathematical: profit = Σ(sell_prices) - Σ(buy_prices).

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with 2D Array O(n*k)
approaches.summary: Use a 2D DP table where dp[t][i] represents the maximum profit with at most t transactions up to day i. Iterate through days and transactions, updating based on previous profits and best buy points. This is a standard DP solution for stock problems with transaction limits.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock IV with DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using 2D DP table to maximize profit with at most k transactions.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the maximum profit achievable by buying and selling a stock at most k times, given daily prices. The dynamic programming approach uses a 2D table dp[t][i] representing the maximum profit with at most t transactions up to day i. The intuition is to break the problem into subproblems: for each transaction count t and day i, we either don't make a transaction on day i (carry profit from previous day) or complete a transaction ending on day i. The latter requires finding the best previous day j to buy, which can be optimized by tracking the maximum of (dp[t-1][j-1] - prices[j]) for all j < i. This avoids O(n²) complexity per transaction. The algorithm initializes dp[0][i] = 0 (no transactions) and dp[t][0] = 0 (only one day). Then, for t from 1 to k and i from 1 to n-1, we update dp[t][i] = max(dp[t][i-1], prices[i] + maxDiff), where maxDiff tracks the best buy point. Time complexity is O(n*k) as we iterate through transactions and days, and space is O(n*k) for the DP table. Connection to L1: This integrates the key subroutine of updating maxDiff efficiently. Common mistakes include not handling k >= n/2 specially (which reduces to unlimited transactions), incorrect initialization, or missing the optimization for maxDiff. Real code pattern: Initialize dp with zeros, loop transactions, maintain maxDiff as -prices[0], update dp and maxDiff iteratively.

examples.trace: n=3, k=2. Initialize dp[3][3] all zeros. Transaction t=1: maxDiff = -prices[0] = -2. Day i=1: dp[1][1] = max(dp[1][0]=0, prices[1]=4 + maxDiff=-2) = max(0,2)=2, update maxDiff = max(maxDiff=-2, dp[0][0]=0 - prices[1]=4) = max(-2,-4)=-2. Day i=2: dp[1][2] = max(dp[1][1]=2, prices[2]=1 + maxDiff=-2) = max(2,-1)=2, update maxDiff = max(maxDiff=-2, dp[0][1]=0 - prices[2]=1) = max(-2,-1)=-1. Transaction t=2: maxDiff = -prices[0] = -2. Day i=1: dp[2][1] = max(dp[2][0]=0, prices[1]=4 + maxDiff=-2) = max(0,2)=2, update maxDiff = max(maxDiff=-2, dp[1][0]=0 - prices[1]=4) = max(-2,-4)=-2. Day i=2: dp[2][2] = max(dp[2][1]=2, prices[2]=1 + maxDiff=-2) = max(2,-1)=2. Final answer dp[2][2]=2.

n=6, k=2. Initialize dp. t=1: maxDiff=-3. i=1: dp[1][1]=max(0,2+(-3)=-1)=0, maxDiff=max(-3,0-2=-2)=-2. i=2: dp[1][2]=max(0,6+(-2)=4)=4, maxDiff=max(-2,0-6=-6)=-2. i=3: dp[1][3]=max(4,5+(-2)=3)=4, maxDiff=max(-2,0-5=-5)=-2. i=4: dp[1][4]=max(4,0+(-2)=-2)=4, maxDiff=max(-2,0-0=0)=0. i=5: dp[1][5]=max(4,3+0=3)=4, maxDiff=max(0,0-3=-3)=0. t=2: maxDiff=-3. i=1: dp[2][1]=max(0,2+(-3)=-1)=0, maxDiff=max(-3,4-2=2)=2. i=2: dp[2][2]=max(0,6+2=8)=8, maxDiff=max(2,4-6=-2)=2. i=3: dp[2][3]=max(8,5+2=7)=8, maxDiff=max(2,4-5=-1)=2. i=4: dp[2][4]=max(8,0+2=2)=8, maxDiff=max(2,4-0=4)=4. i=5: dp[2][5]=max(8,3+4=7)=8. But wait, check: For t=2, i=5, maxDiff should be max(prev maxDiff, dp[1][4] - prices[5]) = max(4, 4-3=1)=4, so dp[2][5]=max(8,3+4=7)=8? Error: Actually, dp[1][4]=4 from above, so correct. However, expected output is 7, let's recompute carefully. t=1: dp[1][2]=4 (buy at 2, sell at 6), dp[1][5]=4 (buy at 0, sell at 3). t=2: For i=5, maxDiff tracks max(dp[1][j-1] - prices[j]) for j<=5. At i=4, maxDiff=4 (from dp[1][3]-prices[4]=4-0=4). At i=5, update maxDiff = max(4, dp[1][4]-prices[5]=4-3=1)=4. Then dp[2][5] = max(dp[2][4]=7, prices[5]+maxDiff=3+4=7)=7. So final is 7. Full trace: t=2: i=1: dp=0, maxDiff=2; i=2: dp=8, maxDiff=2; i=3: dp=8, maxDiff=2; i=4: dp=8, maxDiff=4; i=5: dp=7.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Optimized MaxDiff Update in DP
ladders.desc: Efficiently track the best buy point for each transaction using a running maximum.
ladders.explanation: In the DP solution for stock problems, a critical optimization is avoiding O(n²) time when computing dp[t][i] = max(dp[t][i-1], prices[i] + max_{j<i} (dp[t-1][j-1] - prices[j])). The term max_{j<i} (dp[t-1][j-1] - prices[j]) can be updated incrementally as i increases, rather than recomputed from scratch each time. We maintain a variable maxDiff that represents this maximum value for the current transaction t. Initially, for each t, maxDiff is set to -prices[0] (since dp[t-1][-1] is 0 for j=0). As we iterate through days i from 1 to n-1, we update dp[t][i] using the current maxDiff, and then update maxDiff to max(maxDiff, dp[t-1][i-1] - prices[i]). This reduces the inner loop to O(1) per day. The intuition is that maxDiff accumulates the best possible 'buy point' profit up to the previous day, allowing us to compute the sell at day i efficiently. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(n) per transaction, contributing to the overall O(n*k). Space is O(1) extra. Connection to L0: This subroutine is embedded in the nested loops of the full solution. Common mistakes include initializing maxDiff incorrectly (e.g., to 0), or updating it after dp[t][i] in the wrong order. Real code pattern: For each t, maxDiff = -prices[0]; for i from 1 to n-1: dp[t][i] = max(dp[t][i-1], prices[i] + maxDiff); maxDiff = max(maxDiff, dp[t-1][i-1] - prices[i]).

examples.trace: Initialize maxDiff = -prices[0] = -2. For i=1: Compute dp (not needed here), update maxDiff = max(-2, dp_prev[0]=0 - prices[1]=4) = max(-2,-4) = -2. For i=2: Update maxDiff = max(-2, dp_prev[1]=0 - prices[2]=1) = max(-2,-1) = -1. So maxDiff sequence: [-2, -2, -1].

Initialize maxDiff = -prices[0] = -3. i=1: Update maxDiff = max(-3, dp_prev[0]=0 - prices[1]=2) = max(-3,-2) = -2? Wait, dp_prev[0]=0, so 0-2=-2, max(-3,-2)=-2. But in example, it's 2? Error: Let's correct. For t=2, dp_prev is for t=1. From L0, dp[1] = [0,0,4,4,4,4]. So i=1: maxDiff = max(-3, dp_prev[0]=0 - 2 = -2) = -2. i=2: maxDiff = max(-2, dp_prev[1]=0 - 6 = -6) = -2. i=3: maxDiff = max(-2, dp_prev[2]=4 - 5 = -1) = -1. i=4: maxDiff = max(-1, dp_prev[3]=4 - 0 = 4) = 4. i=5: maxDiff = max(4, dp_prev[4]=4 - 3 = 1) = 4. So sequence: [-3, -2, -2, -1, 4, 4]. But in L0 trace, for t=2, maxDiff was [ -3,2,2,2,4,4]. Discrepancy: In L0, for t=2, i=1, maxDiff updated to 2 because dp_prev[0] is 0? No, dp_prev[0] is 0, so 0-2=-2. Let's re-check L0: For t=2, i=1, maxDiff = max(-3, dp[1][0] - prices[1]) = max(-3, 0-2=-2) = -2, but L0 said 2. That was an error in L0 trace. Correcting: maxDiff for t=2: start -3; i=1: max(-3,0-2=-2)=-2; i=2: max(-2,0-6=-6)=-2; i=3: max(-2,4-5=-1)=-1; i=4: max(-1,4-0=4)=4; i=5: max(4,4-3=1)=4. So output here should be [-3, -2, -2, -1, 4, 4].

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP State Transition for Stock Transactions
ladders.desc: Define DP state and recurrence relation for maximizing profit with limited transactions.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is to define a dynamic programming state dp[t][i] as the maximum profit achievable with at most t transactions up to day i (where days are indexed from 0). The recurrence relation has two cases: 1) No transaction on day i: profit is dp[t][i-1]. 2) Complete a transaction that ends on day i (i.e., sell on day i): we must have bought on some earlier day j < i, so profit is prices[i] - prices[j] + dp[t-1][j-1] (since one transaction is used for this buy-sell pair). To find the best j, we take the maximum over all j < i: dp[t][i] = max(dp[t][i-1], max_{j<i} (prices[i] - prices[j] + dp[t-1][j-1])). This can be rewritten as dp[t][i] = max(dp[t][i-1], prices[i] + max_{j<i} (dp[t-1][j-1] - prices[j])). The term max_{j<i} (dp[t-1][j-1] - prices[j]) is independent of i in the inner loop and can be optimized as in L1. The intuition is that we build profit incrementally, considering whether to include day i in a transaction. Time complexity without optimization is O(n²*k), but with optimization it becomes O(n*k). Space is O(n*k) for the DP table. Connection to L1: The optimization handles the inner max efficiently. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices (e.g., using j instead of j-1 for dp[t-1]), or misunderstanding the state definition. Real code pattern: dp[t][i] = max(dp[t][i-1], prices[i] + maxDiff), where maxDiff is maintained as described.

examples.trace: For t=1, i=1. Option 1: no transaction, dp[1][0] = 0. Option 2: sell on day 1, buy on day j=0: profit = prices[1] - prices[0] + dp[0][-1] (dp[0][-1] is 0 by convention) = 4-2+0=2. So max(0,2)=2. Using formula: maxDiff for j<1: max_{j<1} (dp[0][j-1] - prices[j]) = dp[0][-1] - prices[0] = 0-2 = -2. Then dp[1][1] = max(0, prices[1] + (-2)) = max(0,2)=2.

Assume dp[1] = [0,0,4,4,4,4]. For t=2, i=4. Option 1: no transaction, dp[2][3] = 8 from earlier? Let's compute stepwise. From L0, dp[2][3]=8. Option 2: sell on day 4, buy on best j<4. max_{j<4} (dp[1][j-1] - prices[j]). Compute: j=0: dp[1][-1]=0 - 3 = -3; j=1: dp[1][0]=0 - 2 = -2; j=2: dp[1][1]=0 - 6 = -6; j=3: dp[1][2]=4 - 5 = -1. Max is -1. So dp[2][4] = max(dp[2][3]=8, prices[4]=0 + (-1)) = max(8,-1)=8. But in L0, dp[2][4]=7? Contradiction. Let's use optimized maxDiff: At i=4, maxDiff should be 4 (from L1). Then dp[2][4] = max(dp[2][3], 0+4) = max(8,4)=8. But expected in L0 is 7. Error in L0 trace: dp[2][3] might not be 8. Recompute from scratch: prices=[3,2,6,5,0,3]. dp[0][i]=0. t=1: i=1: dp[1][1]=max(0,2-3+0=-1)=0; i=2: dp[1][2]=max(0,6-2+0=4)=4; i=3: dp[1][3]=max(4,5-6+0=-1)=4; i=4: dp[1][4]=max(4,0-5+0=-5)=4; i=5: dp[1][5]=max(4,3-0+0=3)=4. So dp[1]=[0,0,4,4,4,4]. t=2: i=1: dp[2][1]=max(0,2+maxDiff) with maxDiff initialized to -3? Actually, maxDiff starts at -prices[0]=-3. dp[2][1]=max(0,2+(-3)=-1)=0; update maxDiff = max(-3, dp[1][0]-prices[1]=0-2=-2) = -2. i=2: dp[2][2]=max(0,6+(-2)=4)=4; update maxDiff = max(-2, dp[1][1]-prices[2]=0-6=-6) = -2. i=3: dp[2][3]=max(4,5+(-2)=3)=4; update maxDiff = max(-2, dp[1][2]-prices[3]=4-5=-1) = -1. i=4: dp[2][4]=max(4,0+(-1)=-1)=4; update maxDiff = max(-1, dp[1][3]-prices[4]=4-0=4) = 4. i=5: dp[2][5]=max(4,3+4=7)=7. So dp[2][4]=4, not 7 or 8. Correct output for i=4 is 4. In L0, dp[2][4] was 7? That was for i=5. So for this example, with i=4, dp[2][4]=4.

ladders.title: Building Block — 2D DP Table Initialization and Iteration
ladders.desc: Set up and iterate through a 2D DP table for sequential decision problems.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating a 2D dynamic programming table and iterating through it in a specific order to solve problems with two dimensions, such as transactions and days. The table dp has dimensions (k+1) x n, where k is the max transactions and n is the number of days. Rows represent transaction counts from 0 to k, and columns represent days from 0 to n-1. Initialization: dp[0][i] = 0 for all i (no transactions yield no profit), and dp[t][0] = 0 for all t (with only one day, no transaction can be completed). The iteration order is crucial: we loop transaction count t from 1 to k, and within each t, loop days i from 1 to n-1. This ensures that when computing dp[t][i], the values dp[t][i-1] and dp[t-1][j] for j < i are already computed. The intuition is that we build solutions for smaller subproblems first. Time complexity for this iteration is O(k*n) if each cell computation is O(1). Space is O(k*n) for the table. Connection to L2: This structure implements the state transition by providing the framework to fill dp[t][i]. Common mistakes include incorrect dimensions (off-by-one), wrong iteration order (e.g., days outer loop), or not initializing base cases properly. Real code pattern: Create dp as list of lists with zeros, for t in range(1, k+1): for i in range(1, n): compute dp[t][i].

examples.output: DP table initialized and iteration order
examples.trace: Create dp with 3 rows (t=0,1,2) and 3 columns (i=0,1,2). Initialize: dp[0] = [0,0,0], dp[1][0]=0, dp[2][0]=0. Iteration: t=1: i=1 compute dp[1][1], i=2 compute dp[1][2]. t=2: i=1 compute dp[2][1], i=2 compute dp[2][2]. Order: (1,1), (1,2), (2,1), (2,2).

dp size 2x4. Initialize: dp[0]=[0,0,0,0], dp[1][0]=0. t=1: i=1 compute dp[1][1], i=2 compute dp[1][2], i=3 compute dp[1][3]. Assuming prices=[1,2,3,4] and simple max profit, dp[1][1]=1, dp[1][2]=2, dp[1][3]=3. Final table: row0: [0,0,0,0], row1: [0,1,2,3].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Computing Maximum of Two Values with Update
ladders.desc: Perform max comparison and update a variable in a loop.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two numbers to find the maximum and then updating a variable based on this comparison. In the context of the stock problem, we frequently compute dp[t][i] = max(dp[t][i-1], prices[i] + maxDiff) and update maxDiff = max(maxDiff, dp[t-1][i-1] - prices[i]). The intuition is to make optimal choices at each step: either continue with the previous profit or start a new transaction ending at day i. The max function ensures we select the better option. Updating maxDiff maintains the best possible buy point seen so far. This operation is O(1) time and O(1) space. Connection to L3: This operation is performed inside the nested loops of the DP table iteration. Common mistakes include using min instead of max, or updating variables in the wrong order (e.g., updating maxDiff before using it to compute dp[t][i]). Real code pattern: For each i, current_max = max(prev_profit, new_option); then update tracking_variable = max(tracking_variable, new_value).

examples.trace: Compute current_max = max(3,5) = 5. Then update tracking_var = max(2,4) = 4.
current_max = max(0,-1) = 0. tracking_var = max(-3,-2) = -2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Maximum and Loops
ladders.desc: Find the maximum value in a sequence using iterative comparison.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves iterating through a list of numbers and keeping track of the maximum value seen so far. Start with an initial maximum (often the first element or negative infinity). For each subsequent number, compare it with the current maximum; if the new number is larger, update the maximum. This is a basic algorithmic pattern used in many problems. The intuition is to break down the problem into small steps, making optimal decisions locally. Time complexity is O(n) for n numbers, as each is examined once. Space complexity is O(1) if only the maximum is stored. Connection to L4: This is a simpler version of the max operation used in the DP solution, where we compute max of two values and update a tracking variable. Common mistakes include not initializing the maximum properly (e.g., to 0 when numbers could be negative), or off-by-one errors in loops. Real code pattern: max_val = first_element; for each num in list: if num > max_val: max_val = num.

examples.trace: Start max_val = 3. Compare 1: 1 < 3, max stays 3. Compare 4: 4 > 3, update max to 4. Compare 2: 2 < 4, max stays 4. Final max is 4.
Start max_val = -2. Compare -5: -5 < -2, max stays -2. Compare -1: -1 > -2, update max to -1. Final max is -1.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming Space-Optimized O(n*k)
approaches.summary: Optimize the 2D DP approach by using two arrays of size k+1 to store current and previous transaction states, reducing space usage. This maintains the same logic but minimizes memory overhead.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Space-Optimized DP for Stock Trading with k Transactions
ladders.desc: Complete solution using two arrays to track buy/sell states for at most k transactions.
ladders.explanation: This solution solves the problem of maximizing profit with at most k transactions using dynamic programming with space optimization. The intuition is to track two states for each transaction count: the maximum profit when holding a stock (buy state) and when not holding (sell state). We maintain two arrays of size k+1: 'buy' representing maximum profit after buying at transaction j, and 'sell' representing maximum profit after selling at transaction j. For each price in the sequence, we update these arrays from k down to 1 to avoid transaction interference. The 'buy' state at transaction j is updated as max(previous buy[j], sell[j-1] - price), meaning we either keep the previous buy or buy at current price using profit from previous sell. The 'sell' state is updated as max(previous sell[j], buy[j] + price), meaning we either keep previous sell or sell at current price using current buy profit. This ensures we track the best profit for each transaction count without using O(n*k) space. Time complexity is O(n*k) as we iterate through n prices and k transactions. Space complexity is O(k) for the two arrays. Connection to L1: This integrates the transaction update logic into the full price iteration. Common mistakes include updating arrays in wrong order (should be descending to prevent using same transaction multiple times) or initializing arrays incorrectly (buy should start at -infinity except buy[0]=0). Real code pattern: Initialize buy=[-inf]* (k+1) with buy[0]=0, sell=[0]*(k+1). For each price: for j in range(k,0,-1): buy[j]=max(buy[j], sell[j-1]-price); sell[j]=max(sell[j], buy[j]+price). Return sell[k].

examples.trace: Initialize buy=[0,-inf,-inf,-inf], sell=[0,0,0,0]. Iterate prices: Price 1: Update j=3,2,1: buy[1]=-1, sell[1]=0; buy[2]=-1, sell[2]=0; buy[3]=-1, sell[3]=0. Price 2: buy[1]=-1, sell[1]=1; buy[2]=-1, sell[2]=1; buy[3]=-1, sell[3]=1. Price 4: buy[1]=-1, sell[1]=3; buy[2]=1, sell[2]=3; buy[3]=1, sell[3]=3. Price 2: buy[1]=-1, sell[1]=3; buy[2]=1, sell[2]=3; buy[3]=1, sell[3]=3. Price 5: buy[1]=-1, sell[1]=4; buy[2]=1, sell[2]=5; buy[3]=2, sell[3]=5. Price 7: buy[1]=-1, sell[1]=6; buy[2]=1, sell[2]=7; buy[3]=2, sell[3]=8. Price 2: buy[1]=-1, sell[1]=6; buy[2]=4, sell[2]=7; buy[3]=5, sell[3]=8. Price 4: buy[1]=-1, sell[1]=6; buy[2]=4, sell[2]=8; buy[3]=5, sell[3]=10. Price 9: buy[1]=-1, sell[1]=8; buy[2]=4, sell[2]=13; buy[3]=5, sell[3]=15. Price 0: buy[1]=8, sell[1]=8; buy[2]=13, sell[2]=13; buy[3]=15, sell[3]=15. Final sell[3]=15.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Transaction Update for Given Price
ladders.desc: Update buy and sell arrays for a single price with at most k transactions.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine handles updating the DP arrays for one price in the sequence. The intuition is that for each transaction count j from k down to 1, we decide whether to buy or sell at the current price to maximize profit. We maintain two arrays: buy[j] is the maximum profit after buying at transaction j, and sell[j] is after selling at transaction j. For a given price, we update buy[j] as max(previous buy[j], sell[j-1] - price). This means we either do nothing (keep previous buy) or buy at current price using profit from previous sell (transaction j-1). Then update sell[j] as max(previous sell[j], buy[j] + price), meaning we either do nothing or sell at current price using current buy profit. The descending loop (j from k to 1) ensures we don't use the same transaction multiple times in one day. Time complexity is O(k) per price. Space is O(k) for arrays. Connection to L0: This is the core update repeated for each price in the full solution. Common mistakes include ascending loop (causes double-counting) or incorrect initialization. Real code pattern: for j in range(k,0,-1): buy[j] = max(buy[j], sell[j-1] - price); sell[j] = max(sell[j], buy[j] + price).

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Transition for Buy and Sell Decisions
ladders.desc: The DP recurrence relations that define profit maximization per transaction.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental DP equations that drive the solution. The intuition is to model two states for each transaction count: buy[j] and sell[j]. buy[j] represents the maximum profit after completing j transactions and currently holding a stock (i.e., last action was buy). sell[j] represents maximum profit after completing j transactions and not holding (last action was sell). The recurrences are: buy[j] = max(previous buy[j], sell[j-1] - price) and sell[j] = max(previous sell[j], buy[j] + price). For buy[j], we either carry over previous buy state (do nothing) or buy at current price, which requires profit from previous sell state (sell[j-1]) minus price. For sell[j], we either carry over previous sell or sell at current price, adding price to current buy state. These capture all possibilities: hold, buy, sell. Time and space are O(k) per update. Connection to L1: These equations are implemented in the update loop. Common mistakes include incorrect indices or misunderstanding states. Real code pattern: buy_j = max(buy_j, sell_{j-1} - price); sell_j = max(sell_j, buy_j + price).

examples.trace: buy = max(prev_buy, prev_sell_minus1 - price) = max(-inf, 0 - 3) = -3. This means buying at price 3 gives profit -3 (since we start with 0 profit).
sell = max(prev_sell, current_buy + price) = max(0, -3 + 6) = 3. This means selling at price 6 after buying at 3 gives profit 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Transactions in Descending Order
ladders.desc: Why we update transaction counts from k down to 1 to prevent overlap.
ladders.explanation: This concept explains the necessity of processing transactions in reverse order (j from k to 1) during updates. The intuition is to avoid using the same transaction multiple times in a single day. If we update in ascending order (1 to k), when we compute buy[j] using sell[j-1], sell[j-1] might have been updated earlier in the same day, leading to buying and selling on the same day, which is not allowed. By updating descending, we use the sell[j-1] from the previous day (or previous iteration), ensuring each transaction represents a complete buy-sell pair across different days. This maintains the integrity of transaction counting. Time O(k) per price. Space O(1) extra. Connection to L2: This order ensures the recurrence relations use correct previous values. Common mistakes include ascending order causing inflated profits. Real code pattern: for j in range(k, 0, -1): # updates.

examples.input: k=2, buy=[0,-inf,-inf], sell=[0,0,0], price=3, update ascending
examples.output: Incorrect buy[2] might use updated sell[1]
examples.trace: Ascending: j=1: buy[1]=max(-inf, sell[0]-3)= -3; sell[1]=max(0, buy[1]+3)=0. j=2: buy[2]=max(-inf, sell[1]-3)=max(-inf,0-3)= -3 (uses sell[1] from same day, but sell[1] hasn't changed). Here it's okay, but with multiple prices it can cause issues. Descending ensures independence.

examples.input: k=2, buy=[0,-2,4], sell=[0,4,4], price=0, update descending
examples.trace: Descending: j=2: buy[2]=max(4, sell[1]-0)=4; sell[2]=max(4,4+0)=4. j=1: buy[1]=max(-2, sell[0]-0)=0; sell[1]=max(4,0+0)=4. Uses sell[1] from previous state for j=2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing and Updating Maximum Values
ladders.desc: The fundamental max() operations used in DP updates.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic operation of taking the maximum between two values to update states. The intuition is that at each step, we want to retain the best profit so far, so we use max() to choose between doing nothing (keeping previous state) and taking an action (buying or selling). For example, buy[j] = max(previous buy[j], sell[j-1] - price) compares keeping the previous buy profit versus buying at current price. This ensures we always track the maximum possible profit. This is a core DP technique: optimal substructure where current best depends on previous best. Time O(1) per comparison. Space O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is applied in the descending loop. Common mistakes include using min() or incorrect arithmetic. Real code pattern: max(a, b) where a and b are integers or floats.

examples.trace: max(-inf, -3) = -3. Since -inf is less than any number, we choose -3.
max(0, 3) = 3. We choose the larger profit.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Profit as Difference in Prices
ladders.desc: The basic idea that profit comes from buying low and selling high.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept explains that profit in stock trading is simply the difference between sell price and buy price. For a single transaction, profit = sell_price - buy_price. If you buy at price 3 and sell at 6, profit is 3. For multiple transactions, total profit is the sum of profits from each buy-sell pair. This is intuitive: you make money when you sell at a higher price than you bought. In algorithms, we represent this as arithmetic operations: when buying, we subtract price from current profit (since it's a cost), and when selling, we add price (since it's revenue). This sets the stage for DP where we track these operations. Time O(1) per calculation. Space O(1). Connection to L4: This is why we use subtraction for buy and addition for sell in max operations. Common mistakes include confusing profit with price or not handling negative profits. Real code pattern: profit = sell_price - buy_price.

examples.trace: Profit = sell_price - buy_price = 6 - 3 = 3.
Profit = 10 - 5 = 5.

approaches.name: Greedy with Heap for Large k O(n log n)
approaches.summary: For cases where k is large (e.g., k >= n/2), treat it as unlimited transactions and use a greedy approach with a min-heap to track profitable intervals. This adapts based on k's value to avoid unnecessary DP computations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Greedy with Heap for Large k in Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock IV
ladders.desc: Complete solution using greedy approach with min-heap when k is large (k ≥ n/2), adapting based on k's value.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the 'Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock IV' problem by first checking if k is large enough to treat transactions as unlimited. When k ≥ n/2, we can make at most one transaction per price pair, so we use a greedy strategy with a min-heap to maximize profit. The intuition is that for large k, the constraint becomes irrelevant, and we can focus on capturing all profitable price increases. We maintain a min-heap to track potential buying prices and profits. As we iterate through prices, we push each price into the heap. If the current price is higher than the heap's minimum (lowest buying price), we calculate the profit, pop the minimum, and push the current price back (allowing re-buying at the same price for future transactions). This effectively captures all upward price movements. For small k, we fall back to dynamic programming. The algorithm: 1. If k ≥ n/2, initialize profit = 0 and a min-heap. 2. For each price in prices: push price to heap; if price > heap[0], add profit += price - heap[0], pop heap, push price. 3. Return profit. Time: O(n log n) for heap operations; space: O(n) for heap. Connection: This integrates the greedy logic with k-checking. Common mistakes: Not handling small k separately, incorrect heap usage. Real code pattern: Use heapq in Python, with condition for k.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Greedy Profit Maximization with Min-Heap for Unlimited Transactions
ladders.desc: The helper function that uses a min-heap to compute maximum profit with unlimited transactions.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the greedy approach when transactions are unlimited (or k is large). The intuition is to capture every price increase by maintaining a min-heap of buying prices. As we iterate through prices, we treat each price as a potential selling point. If the current price is higher than the smallest buying price in the heap, we realize a profit by selling at the current price and buying back immediately at the same price (to allow future transactions). This works because in unlimited transactions, we can buy and sell on the same day conceptually. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize profit = 0 and an empty min-heap. 2. For each price in the price array: a. Push the current price onto the heap. b. If the current price is greater than the minimum value in the heap (i.e., heap[0]): i. Add the difference (price - heap[0]) to profit. ii. Pop the minimum from the heap. iii. Push the current price onto the heap (representing re-buying at this price). 3. Return profit. Time complexity: O(n log n) due to heap operations (push and pop are O(log n) each, done for each price). Space complexity: O(n) for the heap in worst case. Connection to L0: This function is called when k is large in the full problem. Common mistakes: Forgetting to push the price after popping, which would miss opportunities for re-buying. Real code pattern: Use heapq module in Python, with heapify for min-heap.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Summing Positive Consecutive Differences for Maximum Profit
ladders.desc: The central insight that maximum profit with unlimited transactions is the sum of all price increases.
ladders.explanation: This core logic reveals that when you can perform unlimited transactions (buy and sell multiple times), the optimal strategy is to buy at every local minimum and sell at every local maximum, which simplifies to summing all positive differences between consecutive days. The intuition is that any price increase represents a profitable transaction, and since there's no limit on transactions, you can capture each increase individually. For example, if prices are [1,3,5], you could buy at 1 and sell at 5 for profit 4, or buy at 1, sell at 3, buy at 3, sell at 5 for profit 2+2=4—the same total. This works because selling and buying on the same day (like at price 3) is allowed in unlimited transactions, effectively splitting larger intervals. The algorithm is straightforward: iterate through the price array, and whenever today's price is higher than yesterday's, add the difference to total profit. Time complexity: O(n) for a single pass. Space complexity: O(1) using only a few variables. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the greedy sub-routine for unlimited transactions. Common mistakes: Trying to find complex patterns or overcomplicating with heaps, when a simple linear scan suffices. Real code pattern: A for-loop from index 1 to n-1, with an if condition to check price increase.

ladders.title: Building Block — Identifying Price Increases in a Sequence
ladders.desc: An intermediate concept of detecting when a price is higher than the previous one in an array.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on comparing each element in an array with its immediate predecessor to identify increases. It's a fundamental step in many financial or time-series analyses. The intuition is that by scanning sequentially, we can pinpoint moments where value rises, which often indicate opportunities (like buying low and selling high). The algorithm: given an array 'prices', iterate from the second element to the last. For each index i, check if prices[i] > prices[i-1]. If true, record this as a price increase; otherwise, it's a decrease or no change. This can be extended to compute the difference or count increases. Time complexity: O(n) for linear traversal. Space complexity: O(1) if only tracking a counter or sum. Connection to L2: This operation is used to sum positive differences for profit calculation. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in loop indices (starting from 0 instead of 1), or misunderstanding equal prices (which are not increases). Real code pattern: Use a for-loop with range(1, len(prices)).

So increases at indices 2 and 4.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Consecutive Elements in an Array
ladders.desc: A fundamental operation of checking if one element is greater than the previous in a sequence.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves taking two adjacent elements from an array and determining their relationship—specifically, whether the later element is greater than the earlier one. It's a simple comparison that forms the basis for many algorithms, such as finding trends or sorting. The intuition is that by comparing neighbors, we can understand local changes in data. The algorithm: given an array A and an index i (where i > 0), access A[i] and A[i-1], then evaluate A[i] > A[i-1]. If true, it indicates an increase; if false, it might be a decrease or equality. This operation requires constant time O(1) and space O(1). Connection to L3: Repeated application of this operation across the array builds up to identifying all price increases. Common mistakes: Using incorrect indices (e.g., i=0 for the first element, which has no predecessor), or misunderstanding array bounds. Real code pattern: In a loop, use if A[i] > A[i-1].

Access A[1]=4 and A[0]=2. Compare: 4 > 2? Yes, so output True.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Accessing Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: The simplest prerequisite of retrieving an element from an array using its position.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves understanding how to access individual elements in an array (or list) by their index. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, and each element has a unique index starting from 0. The intuition is that by knowing the index, we can directly retrieve or modify the value at that position, enabling efficient data manipulation. The operation: given an array A and an integer index i, access A[i] to get the element. For example, if A = [10,20,30], then A[0] = 10, A[1] = 20, A[2] = 30. Time complexity: O(1) for direct access. Space complexity: O(1) for the reference. Connection to L4: This operation is used to get consecutive elements for comparison. Common mistakes: Using indices out of bounds (e.g., negative or beyond array length), or confusing 0-based indexing with 1-based. Real code pattern: In Python, use square brackets like A[i].
